cuisine: Eastern European / Japanese / Global Health Food
food_subgroup: Pseudocereal — cooked buckwheat groats (Fagopyrum esculentum), naturally gluten-free

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cooked buckwheat groats; SNOMED CT coverage for individual cooked pseudocereals is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked buckwheat groats is approximately one cup (168g), providing around 155 kcal, 5.7g protein, and 33g carbohydrate with a low glycemic load.

nutrient_source.title: Buckwheat, cooked — USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170286
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy FDC 170286 — Buckwheat, cooked. All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Buckwheat Groats — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 45 for buckwheat groats sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per 100g) / 100 = (45 × 17.2) / 100 ≈ 7.7. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate (19.9g) minus dietary fibre (2.7g) = 17.2g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4mg/100g), high potassium (160mg/100g) and magnesium (51mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Buckwheat cooked groats are highly suitable for hypertensive patients: sodium content is negligible at 4mg/100g, while potassium (160mg) and magnesium (51mg) per 100g actively support vasodilation and blood pressure regulation; rutin content may additionally strengthen capillary integrity.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (45), low GL (~7.7 per 100g), D-chiro-inositol and rutin content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Buckwheat groats have a low glycemic index of 45 and glycemic load of approximately 7.7 per 100g, making them appropriate for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; D-chiro-inositol (DCI) present in buckwheat has demonstrated insulin-sensitising properties, and rutin modestly inhibits alpha-glucosidase activity, further attenuating postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1g/100g), zero cholesterol, high rutin content, and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Buckwheat groats are cardioprotective: saturated fat is minimal at 0.1g/100g, cholesterol is absent, and rutin (a quercetin glycoside present at approximately 25mg/100g) exerts anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects relevant to endothelial health and atherosclerosis prevention; the high potassium-to-sodium ratio further supports cardiovascular risk reduction.

coeliac_disease.key_factor: Naturally gluten-free pseudocereal — not botanically related to wheat
coeliac_disease.guidance: Buckwheat is naturally gluten-free and safe for patients with coeliac disease and non-coeliac gluten sensitivity; despite its name, buckwheat (Fagopyrum esculentum, family Polygonaceae) has no botanical relationship to wheat (Triticum spp., family Poaceae) and contains no gluten proteins.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rutin anti-inflammatory properties (NF-κB inhibition), high magnesium, gluten-free
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Buckwheat groats may benefit rheumatoid arthritis patients through rutin's NF-κB inhibitory and antioxidant activity, high magnesium content (51mg/100g), and gluten-free status relevant for RA patients with concurrent gluten sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buckwheat Cooked Groats (per 100g): approximately 92 kcal, 3.4g complete protein (containing all 9 essential amino acids including lysine), 0.6g fat (0.1g saturated), 19.9g carbohydrate, 2.7g dietary fibre, and only 4mg sodium. Buckwheat (Fagopyrum esculentum) is a naturally gluten-free pseudocereal belonging to the Polygonaceae family — the same family as rhubarb and sorrel — and is NOT botanically related to wheat despite its common name. This is a critical clinical distinction: patients and clinicians frequently assume buckwheat contains gluten, but it is entirely safe for coeliac disease and non-coeliac gluten sensitivity. Buckwheat groats are the hulled seeds of the buckwheat plant; when roasted before cooking they are known as kasha (Eastern European tradition). Cooked in water, the groats have a soft, slightly chewy texture with a mild, earthy, nutty flavour. Glycemic index is low at approximately 45 (University of Sydney GI Database), with a glycemic load of approximately 7.7 per 100g — making buckwheat suitable for blood glucose management. Buckwheat contains D-chiro-inositol (DCI), which has demonstrated insulin-sensitising properties relevant to Type 2 Diabetes and PCOS management. Rutin (a quercetin glycoside, approximately 25mg/100g) provides anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and capillary-strengthening effects relevant to cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis management. Magnesium content is notable at 51mg/100g. Buckwheat cultivation originated in Central Asia and Northeast China approximately 6,000 years ago, spreading westward to Eastern Europe (kasha tradition) and eastward to Japan (soba noodle tradition). Available in Singapore at Cold Storage organic section and RedMart. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 170286. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: is buckwheat safe for coeliac disease

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170286 (Buckwheat, cooked) — an approved primary source with exact match. GI value of 45 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03303126, FoodEx2 A0BWH) are defensible and directly applicable. No estimation or analogy required for core nutritional data.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC 170286 — buckwheat groats cooked in water contain no added or free sugars. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as buckwheat is a plant-based pseudocereal. vitamin_b12_mcg is naturally zero as buckwheat is a plant food containing no cobalamin. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are confirmed zero or trace per USDA FDC 170286. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FDC 170286. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.18g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.18g) are sourced from USDA FDC 170286 fatty acid profile. water_g (75.97g) sourced from USDA FDC 170286. SNOMED CT code is null: no specific SNOMED CT concept identified for cooked buckwheat groats; SNOMED CT coverage for individual cooked pseudocereals is limited as of 2024. GI glycemic load calculated as (45 × 17.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.7, where available carbohydrate = 19.9g total carbohydrate minus 2.7g dietary fibre = 17.2g. Buckwheat is NOT related to wheat — it is a pseudocereal in the Polygonaceae family, safe for coeliac disease. Tamil and Hindi local names are not established for buckwheat in Singapore culinary tradition; this food is not a Singaporean dish and the multilingual G6 requirement for Tamil/Hindi applies to Singaporean dishes specifically — buckwheat is classified as Eastern European / Japanese / Global Health Food cuisine and does not trigger the Singaporean multilingual requirement.
